name: Jerusalem Temple priesthood
shortDescription: The Jerusalem Temple priesthood were the ordained individuals responsible for maintaining and officiating religious services in the Temple in Jerusalem. Their roles encompassed sacrifices, rituals, and the interpretation of Jewish law.
description: The Jerusalem Temple priesthood held a central role in ancient Jewish religious life, responsible for conducting the daily and festival services within the Temple in Jerusalem. Their duties included performing sacrifices, maintaining the Temple's purity, reciting prayers, and teaching the Torah. The priesthood was traditionally hereditary, descending from Aaron, the brother of Moses, and was divided into 24 priestly divisions (mishmarot) that served in rotation. The High Priest, chosen from among the priests, held the highest religious authority and officiated on Yom Kippur. The Temple priesthood's authority and practices are described in detail within the Hebrew Bible, particularly in the books of Leviticus and Numbers. The priesthood's calendar and ritual practices, including the calendar of festivals and sacrifices, played a crucial role in structuring Jewish time and community life. The destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE marked a significant turning point in Jewish history, leading to the cessation of Temple sacrifices and a shift towards rabbinic Judaism, which emphasized prayer, study, and community-based observance. The memory of the Temple and its priesthood remains central to Jewish identity and longing for restoration.

sources.title: The History of the Jewish People in the Age of Jesus Christ (175 B.C. - A.D. 135)
sources.author: Emil Schürer

sources.title: Ancient Israel: Its Life and Institutions
sources.author: Roland de Vaux
